stimulus: The vibrations created by an obstruction in flowing water produce eddies (rotating currents) of varying size; by sensing these eddies through their lateral lines, fish can determine the obstruction’s size and location. Laboratory work by Tomás Vega-Reyes and colleagues using fish models of three body shapes—slender, intermediate, and stocky—showed that for small eddies, fish with stocky body shapes would be least able to distinguish between the eddies and the surrounding turbulence. A second research team has therefore hypothesized that, in murky low-visibility water, stocky-bodied fish will be less likely than slender-bodied fish to detect obstructions.
stem: Which finding, if true, would most directly support the second research team’s hypothesis?

answerOptions.content: A study using obstructions that produced small eddies in murky water found that the stocky-bodied bullhead catfish collided with obstructions more often than the slender-bodied longfin chub did.
A study using obstructions that produced small eddies in murky water found that the slender-bodied longfin chub collided with obstructions about as often as the stocky-bodied bullhead catfish did.

A study using obstructions that produced small eddies in murky water found that some specimens of the slender-bodied longfin chub collided with obstructions more often than other specimens of the same species did.
A study using obstructions that produced small eddies in murky water found that the stocky-bodied bullhead catfish collided with more than half of the obstructions presented.

rationale: Choice A is the best answer because it provides a direct between-species comparison in the exact conditions the hypothesis predicts: stocky-bodied fish collide more often than slender-bodied fish in low-visibility water with small eddies. Choice B contradicts the hypothesis by showing no difference. Choice C describes within-species variation, not the body-shape contrast. Choice D gives only the stocky-bodied performance without comparison.
